one_line_definition: The phase velocity of an acoustic wave propagating through a metallic medium, determined by elastic moduli and mass density
The change in acoustic wave velocity (or attenuation) caused by an externally applied static magnetic field interacting with conduction electrons
Macroscopic electromagnetic-acoustic theory predicting ΔC ∝ H² via a Hall-current-like force on conduction electrons
Characteristic depth over which an electromagnetic wave decays in a conductor; δ = (c²/2πωσ)^(1/2)
Average distance an electron travels between scattering events; determines whether macroscopic theory applies (l ≪ λ required)
Ultrasonic pulse-transit technique where transit time becomes the period of a feedback oscillator, enabling ppm-level velocity measurement
Electrons whose cyclotron orbits in k-space are not closed, tracing paths across Brillouin zone boundaries; associated with anomalous magnetoresistance
Angular frequency of circular electron motion in a magnetic field: ωc = eH/mc
The constant-energy surface in momentum space separating filled from empty electron states; topology determines transport anisotropy
Current component perpendicular to both the charge carrier velocity and the applied magnetic field, arising from the Lorentz force
Dependence of velocity change on the angle θ between acoustic propagation direction and magnetic field: sin²θ for longitudinal, cos²θ for shear modes
Acoustic mode in which particle displacement is parallel to the propagation direction
Acoustic mode in which particle displacement is perpendicular to the propagation direction
Direction-dependent change in electrical resistivity under an applied magnetic field, sensitive to Fermi-surface open orbits
Acoustic measurements in oriented single crystals where propagation along principal axes guarantees pure longitudinal or pure shear modes
